questions.question: Explain the difference between total participation and partial participation in a relationship, and provide an example from a university database where each type occurs.
keyPoints: Total participation means every entity in the set must participate in the relationship (e.g., each Student must have an Enrollment).
Partial participation allows some entities to not participate (e.g., an Instructor may not teach a Course).
In ER diagrams total participation is shown by a double line, partial by a single line.
Total participation often leads to a foreign key defined with NOT NULL.

questions.question: Define referential integrity actions ON DELETE and ON UPDATE. Discuss the behavior of CASCADE, SET NULL, and NO ACTION with examples.
keyPoints: Referential integrity actions specify what happens to dependent rows when a referenced row is deleted or updated.
CASCADE automatically deletes or updates matching dependent rows.
SET NULL sets the foreign key columns to NULL, provided the column is nullable.
NO ACTION aborts the operation if dependent rows exist, preserving integrity.

questions.question: Design a relational schema for a library system that includes entities Member, Book, Loan, and Author. Specify primary keys, foreign keys, cardinality (1:N, M:N), participation constraints, and referential integrity actions (ON DELETE CASCADE, ON UPDATE SET NULL). Also, write SQL CHECK constraints to ensure Loan date does not exceed Return date and Member age is at least 18.
keyPoints: Member(MemberID PK, Name, Age CHECK Age>=18, ...); Book(BookID PK, Title, ...); Author(AuthorID PK, Name); BookAuthor(BookID, AuthorID) for M:N.
Loan(LoanID PK, MemberID FK references Member ON DELETE CASCADE, BookID FK references Book ON DELETE CASCADE, LoanDate, ReturnDate CHECK ReturnDate >= LoanDate).
Cardinalities: Member to Loan 1:N, Book to Loan 1:N, Book to Author M:N via BookAuthor.
Participation: each Loan must have a Member and Book (total), but a Member may have no Loan (partial); Author may have no Book (partial).

questions.question: Consider an ER diagram for an online ordering system with entities Customer, Order, Product, and Payment. Identify all relationship constraints: cardinality, participation, weak entity (Order), and referential integrity actions. Explain how you would enforce NOT NULL, UNIQUE, and CHECK constraints at the relational level, and discuss the impact of using ON DELETE SET NULL versus ON DELETE CASCADE on the Order–Product relationship.
keyPoints: Customer(CustomerID PK, Email UNIQUE, Name NOT NULL); Order(OrderID PK, CustomerID FK ON DELETE CASCADE, OrderDate NOT NULL, TotalAmount CHECK TotalAmount>=0).
Order–Product many-to-many realised as OrderItem(OrderID, ProductID FK, Quantity, Subtotal). Order is weak identified by CustomerID and OrderID.
Participation: each Order must have a Customer (total), but a Customer may have no Order (partial); each Product may appear in zero or many OrderItems.
ON DELETE CASCADE removes OrderItem rows when Product is deleted, preserving integrity; ON DELETE SET NULL would leave NULL ProductID, which is undesirable unless ProductID is nullable.
